slides.title: Causal Inference in Observational Health Data
slides.subtitle: PhD Thesis Defense

slides.label: Chapter 1
slides.title: Research Question
slides.subtitle: Can we recover causal treatment effects from electronic health records without randomization?

items.title: Confounding
items.desc: Treatment assignment correlates with patient severity — naïve comparison is biased

items.title: Missing Data
items.desc: EHR data is collected for clinical care, not research — missingness is non-random

items.title: Time-Varying
items.desc: Treatment decisions change over time in response to evolving patient status

slides.label: Dataset
slides.heading: Study Population
slides.desc: Retrospective cohort from three academic medical centers, 2018–2025.

steps.title: Extract
steps.desc: Structured EHR data + NLP on clinical notes

right.title: Sensitivity Analysis
points: Results stable across 5 Super Learner specifications
Missing data mechanism: MAR assumption supported
Propensity score overlap: sufficient across all strata

slides.label: Contributions
slides.heading: Methodological Contributions
slides.desc: Novel extensions to existing causal inference methods.

slides.label: Implications
slides.heading: Clinical and Policy Impact
points: Method enables causal learning from routine clinical data
Reduces dependence on expensive RCTs for evidence generation
Framework is generalizable to other treatment comparisons
Open-source R package: causalEHR v1.2 on CRAN

slides.text: The randomized trial remains the gold standard — but when we can recover the same answer from data that already exists, we owe it to patients to try.
Causal Inference in Observational Health Data · PhD Defense
